One day, I was working deep in a coal mine. It was dark and dusty. Then I heard two men talking nearby. I moved quietly closer to listen.

They were talking about a school in Virginia. It was called Hampton Institute. At this school, poor students could work to pay for their education. They could learn a trade and get a real education at the same time.

As I listened, my heart started to race. That school sounded like the greatest place on earth. Right then, I made a decision: I would go to Hampton. I had no idea where it was. I had no money. I did not even know how far away it was. But I would get there somehow.

I left the mine and found work as a servant for a strict woman named Mrs. Ruffner. Everyone said she was too hard to please. Most boys quit after a few weeks. But I stayed. I learned what she wanted: clean rooms, honest work, and no excuses. She trusted me, and she let me go to school for one hour each day. I studied at night too, by myself, when the house was quiet.

After about a year and a half, I saved enough to begin my journey to Hampton. My mother was sick and worried. She hugged me goodbye, not knowing if she would see me again. My brother gave me what little money he could. The older people in my community gave me nickels and quarters. They had been slaves themselves. They never thought they would see one of their own leave home to attend a boarding school.

The journey was five hundred miles. I did not have enough money for the whole trip. One night, the stagecoach stopped at a small hotel in the mountains. All the other passengers were white. They got warm rooms and hot food. The man at the desk looked at me and refused. No room. No food. I walked the cold streets to stay warm. But I did not feel bitterness. I only thought about Hampton.

Days later, I arrived in the city of Richmond, Virginia — still eighty-two miles from Hampton. I was exhausted. I was hungry. I had no money and no place to sleep. I walked through the city all night. I could smell fried chicken and apple pies from food stands, but I could not afford any of it.

At midnight, I found a spot where the wooden sidewalk was raised above the ground. I crawled underneath and slept on the dirt, using my small bag as a pillow. I could hear people walking above me all night.

The next morning, I saw a ship unloading iron near the dock. I asked the captain if I could help in exchange for money. He agreed. I worked hard for several days. I ate breakfast with my first earned coin. It was the best meal I had ever tasted. I slept under that same sidewalk every night to save money.

When I had saved enough, I walked the rest of the way to Hampton. I arrived with exactly fifty cents.

Then I saw it: a large, beautiful brick building, three stories tall. I had never seen anything so magnificent in my life. I felt something shift inside me. A new life was beginning.

I walked to the head teacher to ask to be admitted as a student. I was dirty. My clothes were worn. I had been traveling for weeks. She looked at me with doubt on her face. She said nothing for a long time.

Finally, she pointed to a room nearby. "That room needs sweeping. Take a broom and sweep it."

I understood immediately. This was my chance — and my test. I swept that room three times. I dusted every surface four times. I moved every desk, every bench. I cleaned every corner and every closet. Mrs. Ruffner had taught me well.

When I finished, the teacher came in with a white handkerchief. She rubbed it along the walls, across the tables, along the floor. She could not find one speck of dirt.

She looked at me and said quietly, "I guess you will do to enter this institution."

I was one of the happiest people on earth. That broom was my entrance exam. I had passed.
